one_line_takeaway: HackSynth is a minimal two-module (Planner + Summarizer) autonomous CTF/pentest agent plus a 200-challenge PicoCTF/OverTheWire benchmark with dynamic solver-generated flags; GPT-4o leads (34% PicoCTF, 40% OverTheWire) and the study doubles as an argument that temperature<=1 and container+firewall isolation are necessary safety controls for command-executing offensive agents.
key_finding: GPT-4o was the strongest base model, solving 41/120 (34.2%) PicoCTF challenges and 32/80 (40.0%) OverTheWire challenges, and the authors note this exceeds what GPT-4o's system card would suggest. The best locally-run model was Llama-3.1-70B (22.5% PicoCTF, 28.8% OverTheWire), beating GPT-4o-mini on both. Safety findings drive the parameter guidance: performance is stable for temperature in [0,1] but degrades and error rates climb above it, and at temperature 2 the environment was consistently rendered unusable before 100 challenges were finished, so temperature <=1 is recommended for deployment. Enabling sampling raised performance by 38%, while prompt-chaining reduced performance by 5% and increased time by 17%. Binary Exploitation was effectively unsolved (0.0% for every model on both benchmarks). Model-specific risk signals emerged: Qwen2-72B frequently issued sudo (19% of commands), and GPT-4o's most common 'output' was a refusal to emit a command for ethical reasons (denoted null/no-tag, ~15%). Cost: GPT-4o ran ~24 cents/challenge vs. <2 cents for GPT-4o-mini.
core_contribution: The paper introduces HackSynth, an autonomous LLM-based penetration-testing agent built from two LLM modules: a Planner that emits a single terminal-executable command (wrapped in <CMD></CMD> tags) inside a containerized Kali Linux environment, and a Summarizer that condenses each command's output into a rolling history that is fed back to the Planner. This loop runs autonomously (up to 20 planning/summarizing steps per challenge) until the flag is captured or the step limit is hit. The second contribution is two new, publicly released CTF benchmarks totaling 200 hand-picked challenges: a PicoCTF set (120 challenges) and an OverTheWire set (80 challenges drawn from the Bandit, Natas, Leviathan, and Krypton wargames), each spanning six categories (General Skills, Cryptography, Web Exploitation, Forensics, Reverse Engineering, Binary Exploitation) and three difficulty levels. Distinctively, the benchmarks ship heuristic solver functions per challenge that programmatically regenerate flags, so the benchmark stays valid even though PicoCTF/OverTheWire flags vary per user and over time (which also resists LLM training-data memorization). The third contribution is a safety/behavioral analysis of the agent's failure modes and containment.
threat_model: This is a dual-use offensive-security paper, so the 'threat model' is twofold. (1) Capability side: the agent is itself an offensive tool that could autonomously exploit vulnerable systems; the authors frame LLM pentest agents as a 'double-edged sword' aiding both attackers and defenders. (2) Agent-safety side (the paper's own risk focus): an autonomous command-executing agent can misbehave against its operator/environment. Documented risks include hallucinating target IP addresses and launching nmap scans against out-of-scope or non-existent hosts, destructive local host actions (e.g. rm -rf, deleting/relocating binaries, altering PATH/env vars), memory exhaustion from unzipping large archives (container crash), and searching for the flag in its own execution environment (raising sandbox-escape concerns). Mitigations: an isolated container plus a whitelist firewall restricting network access to the designated target, with firewall rules re-applied before each generated command. The authors explicitly note the containment is imperfect (an agent with enough privilege could reset firewall rules via a cron job, or pivot through an internet-connected target machine).
limitations: Containment is admittedly incomplete: the whitelist firewall can be bypassed via a scheduled cron job resetting rules or by routing attacks through an internet-connected target. The benchmarks use non-instanced, relatively simple CTF challenges (instanced/CAPTCHA-protected PicoCTF challenges were excluded), which the authors acknowledge are less realistic than full vulnerable VMs (HackTheBox/TryHackMe are named as future targets). Binary exploitation performance is essentially zero across all eight models, so capability is uneven. The agent exhibits a 'rabbit hole' failure mode, persisting on its first-chosen strategy (e.g. repeated base64 decoding) rather than pivoting. Speed/cost comparisons are confounded by variable API response times. Only two small models were used for the parameter sweep, so tuned values may not transfer to all eight benchmarked models. No comparison to prior agents (PentestGPT, Enigma, AutoAttacker, Cybench, NYU CTF) is run — those are discussed only as related work.

datasets_used: PicoCTF benchmark (authors' new set, 120 hand-picked challenges, 6 categories, 3 difficulties, with per-challenge solver functions; 104 challenges carry 184 hints; 40 overlap with Intercode CTF)
OverTheWire benchmark (authors' new set, 80 challenges across the Bandit, Natas, Leviathan, and Krypton wargames)
quantitative_results: GPT-4o: 34.2% (41/120) on PicoCTF, 40.0% (32/80) on OverTheWire — best overall
Llama-3.1-70B: 22.5% PicoCTF, 28.8% OverTheWire — best local model
Qwen2-72B: 20.8% PicoCTF, 25.0% OverTheWire (sudo = 19% of its commands; 8.9% error rate)
Phi-3-mini: 5.0% PicoCTF, 8.8% OverTheWire (weakest; 27% of outputs failed to produce <CMD> tags)
Binary Exploitation category: 0.0% for all 8 models on both benchmarks
GPT-4o PicoCTF category breakdown: General 67.6%, Forensics 23.8%, Crypto 21.7%, Web 14.3%, Reverse Eng 26.3%
Enabling sampling improved performance by 38%; prompt-chaining reduced performance by 5% and increased time by 17%
At temperature 2 the environment was consistently rendered unusable before completing 100 challenges; temperature <=1 recommended
Optimal new-observation-window size ~250 chars (PicoCTF), 500 chars (OverTheWire); reducing window 500->100 chars changed total token usage by <5%
Cost per challenge: GPT-4o ~24 cents, GPT-4o-mini <2 cents; GPT-4o fastest at ~5 min/challenge (PicoCTF), ~2 min (OverTheWire)
GPT-4o's most frequent 'output' on PicoCTF was a no-command/ethical refusal (~15%)
baselines_compared: Comparison is across 8 base LLMs driving the same HackSynth agent: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B, Qwen2-72B, Mixtral-8x7B, Phi-3-mini-4k, Phi-3.5-MoE
NOTE: prior agents PentestGPT, HackingBuddyGPT, AutoAttacker, Enigma, Cybench, and NYU CTF agent are discussed as related work only and are NOT run as empirical baselines in this paper
